(13) Learning Hexo from Scratch - Themes: How to Pick a Hexo Theme
Now we're heading into the themes chapter — but before we get into themes, there's some context worth talking through first.
Picking a theme is something a lot of people get stuck on. Hexo's official site has a

section with around a hundred or so themes, which sounds great, but the details to watch out for are actually more numerous. Picking a theme is kind of like coming up with a username in a game — you sit there forever and still can't decide.

And once you realise you've spent too long on it, you just start picking ridiculous nonsense names. Then once you've actually used the dumb name and realised how cringey it is, you're left with two options:
Delete the character and start over

Picking a Hexo theme works the same way. If you don't pick carefully, on top of having to redo your SEO, you might also end up reshopping for a theme. So when you pick, don't treat it like picking a stupid game username — especially if you go with something embarrassing, because down the line you'll probably regret it just as hard.
There are really only two things that matter:
Is the theme still being actively maintained?
Does it have enough community discussion?

The maintenance question matters because Hexo 4 has better compile performance than Hexo 3. If a theme isn't being maintained, it might not compile against newer Hexo versions, and you'll be stuck — unless you have the chops to fork and modify the theme yourself.
For community discussion: if you pick an obscure theme, even tiny issues become painful, because you might end up talking to Ms. Google for days trying to figure out trivial problems.

If you've got the skills and you've already studied Hexo theme development, the theme doesn't really matter to you — you'll just modify whatever. But if you're lazy like me, stick with the popular themes.

Popular themes usually have lots of Issues — the more people use them, the more weird stuff comes up (?). For example, one of the more popular Hexo themes is NexT (more on that later), and its Issues count is pretty hefty:
Popular themes also tend to have more GitHub stars:
Pick along these lines and you should mostly avoid landmines. Trust me — when a theme stops being maintained or is too obscure, hitting any issue is genuinely painful.
That's it for today's Ironman post. Starting next post, I'll cover how to install and configure themes.
